text: GBV Referral Pathway: CHW Job Aid

GBV means 'gender-based violence'. It includes rape (forced sex), physical violence from a partner or family member, forced marriage, and sexual harassment. GBV can happen to women, men, girls, and boys — but it mostly happens to women and girls. GBV is never the fault of the person it happens to.
What Is GBV?

Types of GBV you may see in your community: (1) Rape or sexual assault — any forced sexual contact, (2) Intimate partner violence — hitting, kicking, threatening by a husband or partner, (3) Child marriage — a girl forced to marry before she is 18, (4) Sexual exploitation — when someone uses power or money to get sex, (5) Harmful practices — cutting, burning, or hurting a person based on tradition.
Your Role as a CHW

As a CHW, you may be the first person a survivor tells. Your response can make a big difference. You do not need to solve the problem or investigate what happened. Your job is to: listen without judging, believe what you are told, keep it private, and connect the person to help.
You are NOT the police. You are NOT a counsellor. You do NOT need to collect a detailed story of what happened. You just need to listen with care, keep it private, and refer to the right place.
How to Listen and Help

When someone tells you about GBV: (1) Find a private place to talk. (2) Sit down and listen calmly. (3) Say: 'I believe you. Thank you for telling me. This is not your fault.' (4) Ask: 'Are you safe right now? Do you need help right away?' (5) Ask: 'What do you need most — help to be safe, medical care, someone to talk to, legal help?' (6) Explain what services are available and let the person choose.
Listen more than you talk. Do not ask 'Why did you stay?' or 'Why did you go there?' or 'What were you wearing?' These questions blame the survivor. Believe what you are told.
What NOT to Do or Say

Do not: (1) Tell anyone else what you heard without the survivor's agreement. (2) Bring family members or community leaders without being asked. (3) Pressure the person to report to police or courts. (4) Ask for details of what happened that are not needed. (5) Make promises you cannot keep (e.g., 'He will go to jail'). (6) Show shock, disgust, or disbelief. (7) Leave the person without giving them information about where to get help.
Safety First

Before you refer, check: Is the person safe right now? Is the person at risk of more harm? If yes, safety comes first. Ask: 'Is he in the house now? Is there somewhere safe you can go tonight?' Help the person think of a safe place — a relative's house, a women's shelter, a religious institution. Do not send a person back into danger.
If the person is in immediate danger: go with them or find a trusted person to go with them. Do not leave them alone until they are safe.
Where to Refer — Urgent Services (Go Today)

Medical care after rape must happen as soon as possible — WITHIN 72 HOURS (3 days) for medicine to prevent HIV to work. WITHIN 5 DAYS (120 hours) for emergency contraception to work. Do not delay. Go to the health centre or hospital today.

What the health centre can do: treat injuries, give medicine to prevent HIV (PEP), give emergency contraception (EC) to prevent pregnancy, treat STIs, give a medical report if the person wants one for legal purposes, connect to a counsellor.

Where to go for other help: (1) GBV caseworker or social worker — for safety planning, emotional support, and connecting to services. (2) Legal aid — if the person wants to report to police or courts. (3) Women's shelter — for a safe place to stay. Ask your supervisor for the contact list for your area.
Following Up

If the person agrees, follow up after 1 week. Check: Did they get the services they needed? Are they safe? Do they need more help? Some survivors take time to decide what they want to do. Be patient. Do not pressure them. Keep following up with kindness if they want you to.

⚠️ Remember: GBV is confidential. Do not discuss it with other community members, other CHWs, or family members of the survivor without the survivor's clear agreement. Even telling 'just one person' can put the survivor at risk.
